replies.text:
They completely missed the mark on what a TV show like The Acolyte should have been.

1) A spy (James Bond-like) TV show set in the Star Wars universe. With a The Sopranos/Breaking Bad-style with the descent into the darkside.
2) Center the story on the Acolyte. The Jedi should absolutely not have been at the center of events. Only show one or brief encounter without a fight (or a final season fight). 
3) Show the toxic relationship between the Sith and his/her Acolyte. The power games (politics, economics, trade market, financing terrorist groups etc). It would have been super interesting, for example, if the Acolyte had sided with "a progressive group" and corrupted them into becoming terrorists. To divide the republic. (In Dune: Prophecy, I really liked how they showed us the double game of the Bene Gesserit sisters)

Optional 4) The search for lost Sith and Jedi knowledges.
------
And it's not hard to understand "THE ACOLYTE". The title focuses on the acolyte, meaning the apprentice of a Sith. Any scriptwriter who has had even a minimal interest in Star Wars and followed the evolution of HBO TV shows would have understood the potential of a title like this.
And Disney has money. They could and should have invested in hiring competent writers.

author: @SpiceCh
text:
12:03 - "The best part about Star Wars is there is no good or evil"

There's only two scenarios that explains this statement: either he never watched Star Wars, or he's trying to argue that the resistance killing clone troops is just as bad as the Empire destroying entire planets in order to establish a rule through fear and oppression.

replies.author: @theoparke
replies.text: Anyone who thinks it's good that "there is no good or evil" is on the side of evil.

replies.author: @SpiceCh
replies.text:
​@theoparke it's not always so black and white, but it certainly is in star wars.

Shows like Arcane do a great job at exploring how "evil" can be misunderstood and arise from multiple people working for their own greater goods coming into conflict with one another. But Emperor Palpatine isn't working for some greater good - he's driven by a classical evil lust for power, and the Empire, being an extension of his will, is therefore as evil as he is. At most one might argue that many of the menial workers and officers in the empire are acting out of fear of personal repercussions should they disobey their orders, but that doesn't redeem their actions and especially not the actions of their leaders.

replies.text: @SpiceCh Can't disagree. On point.
